metadata.title: Relativistic Effects Near Black Holes
metadata.description: Comprehensive database of all relativistic phenomena relevant to black hole visualization: Doppler effects, redshift, time dilation, frame dragging, tidal forces, and more

specialRelativisticDoppler.description: Doppler effect for a source moving relativistically relative to the observer

where.theta: Angle between velocity vector and line of sight (θ=0 means approaching)

relativisticBeaming.description: Relativistic beaming (Doppler boosting) causes the observed intensity to be enhanced or diminished by a power of the Doppler factor

where.alpha: Spectral index (F_ν ∝ ν^(-α)); for thermal emission, often simplified to D^4

intensityFormula.explanation: The power 3 comes from relativistic aberration (solid angle transformation) and frequency shift; +α from the spectrum

thermalCase.note: For blackbody/thermal emission where the spectral shape self-adjusts

fluxFormula.formula: F_obs = D^(3+α) × F_emit (for a point source)
fluxFormula.note: Extended sources get D^(2+α) because of the different solid angle scaling

beamingInAccretionDisks.description: In a rotating accretion disk viewed at some inclination, the approaching side is beamed (brighter) and the receding side is de-beamed (dimmer)
beamingInAccretionDisks.M87observation: The brightness asymmetry in the EHT image of M87* is consistent with Doppler beaming of the approaching side of the accretion flow

jetBeaming.description: Relativistic jets appear dramatically brighter when pointed toward the observer

gravitationalRedshift.description: Photons climbing out of a gravitational well lose energy and are redshifted
gravitationalRedshift.kerrFormula: 1 + z_g depends on position, angular momentum, and spin — full expression involves gtt, gtφ, gφφ metric components

totalRedshift_accretionDisk.note: The observed redshift is the combined gravitational and kinematic (Doppler) contribution

gravitationalTimeDilation.description: Clocks run slower in stronger gravitational fields. τ is proper time (local clock), t is coordinate time (distant observer clock).

interstellarMillerPlanet.scenario: 1 hour on the planet = 7 Earth years
interstellarMillerPlanet.note: Requires near-extremal Kerr BH (a/M = 0.99999999785) with the planet essentially at the horizon. Kip Thorne confirmed this requires spin >0.9999999 in the Interstellar science book.

lenseThirring.name: Lense-Thirring Effect (Frame Dragging)
lenseThirring.alternateFormula: Ω_LT = 2aMc / (r³) in natural units where J = aMc

where.J: Angular momentum of the black hole (J = aGM²/c)
where.Omega_LT: Angular velocity of frame dragging (radians per second)

lenseThirring.description: A rotating mass drags the local inertial frames. Near a Kerr BH, everything — even light — is forced to co-rotate.

horizonAngularVelocity.formula: Ω_H = a c / (2M r_+) = a c³ / (2GM(r_+ + a²/r_+))
horizonAngularVelocity.simplifiedKerr: Ω_H = a / (2M r_+) in natural units

stellarBH_ISCO.note: Extremely rapid frame dragging near stellar mass BHs

geodeticPrecession.description: A gyroscope in orbit around a mass precesses due to the curvature of spacetime (de Sitter precession)

shapiroTimeDelay.description: Light passing near a massive object is delayed compared to the straight-line travel time

solarExample.note: Maximum Shapiro delay for radar signals passing near the Sun

pulsarBinaryExample.note: Most precise test of Shapiro delay

schwarzschildPrecession.perOrbit_rad: 6πM/p where p = a(1-e²) is the semi-latus rectum
schwarzschildPrecession.description: The pericenter of an orbit advances by this amount each orbit

examples.note: Einstein's first successful prediction of GR (1915)

gravitationalWaves.description: Inspiraling objects near black holes emit gravitational waves, losing energy and spiraling inward

mergerSignals.note: First direct detection of gravitational waves (2015)
First multi-messenger detection (GW + EM: kilonova, short GRB)
First intermediate-mass black hole from merger

chirpSignal.description: The GW frequency increases (chirps) as the objects spiral closer

penroseProcess.description: Energy extraction from a rotating black hole by splitting a particle in the ergosphere
mechanism: One fragment falls into the BH with negative energy (as measured at infinity)
The other escapes with MORE energy than the original particle
Net result: energy extracted from BH rotational energy

maxEfficiency.formula: ΔE/E = 1 - 1/√2 ≈ 0.2071 for a single split in extremal Kerr
maxEfficiency.note: This is per event; repeated extraction can drain significant rotational energy

superradiance.description: Wave analog of the Penrose process: waves scattered off a Kerr BH can be amplified
superradiance.condition: ω < m Ω_H where ω = wave frequency, m = azimuthal number, Ω_H = horizon angular velocity
superradiance.amplification: Up to ~138% for electromagnetic waves, ~4.4% for gravitational waves

blandfordZnajekConnection.description: The Blandford-Znajek process is essentially a continuous electromagnetic version of the Penrose process
blandfordZnajekConnection.note: This is how relativistic jets extract energy from spinning BHs

tidalForcesAndSpaghettification.description: The differential gravitational acceleration across an extended body of length d at distance r from mass M

thresholds.label: Lethal (100g across body)
thresholds.note_for_SMBH: Inside the event horizon — you cross the horizon alive

humanSpaghettification.keyInsight: For supermassive BHs (M > ~10⁸ M_sun), a human can cross the event horizon without experiencing lethal tidal forces. Spaghettification occurs later, deep inside.

fromEventHorizon_freefall.note: For a freely falling observer starting from rest at the horizon

coordinateTimeVsProperTime.description: A distant observer sees the infalling object slow down and freeze at the horizon (coordinate time → ∞), but the falling object crosses the horizon in finite proper time
coordinateTimeVsProperTime.note: This is the origin of the historical term 'frozen star' for black holes

seesObjectFalling: Object appears to slow down as it approaches the horizon
Object appears frozen at the horizon (never crosses in finite coordinate time)
Object fades to black as its light is exponentially redshifted

experiences: Crosses the event horizon without any local drama (no wall, no boundary)
Cannot detect the horizon crossing through any local experiment
Tidal forces increase as the singularity approaches
Reaches the singularity in finite proper time (see above)
Cannot send any signal back to the outside after crossing
infallingObserver.note: The equivalence principle guarantees that locally, spacetime always looks flat — there is nothing special about the horizon itself

sources: Hartle, 'Gravity: An Introduction to Einstein's General Relativity' (2003)
Everitt et al., PRL 106, 221101 (2011) — Gravity Probe B
